topic: Christianity: Beliefs, Marriage & the Family, Living the Christian Life, Matters of Life & Death (Spec A)
subject: religious-studies

items.q: What is the doctrine of the Trinity?
items.correct: God is one being existing in three persons (Father, Son, Holy Spirit) — distinct in person but united in essence; Christians remain monotheistic.
items.misconception: Christians believe in three different gods.
items.random_wrong: The Trinity refers to heaven, earth and hell.
items.why: Students treat 'three persons' as 'three gods'. The orthodox position is one God in three distinct persons sharing one substance — a notoriously difficult concept students often misrepresent as polytheism.

items.q: What do Christians mean by resurrection, and how does it differ from reincarnation?
items.correct: Resurrection is the once-for-all bodily raising to eternal life at the end of time (modelled on Christ's resurrection); reincarnation is the soul's repeated rebirth into new bodies. They are distinct doctrines from different religious traditions.
items.misconception: Resurrection and reincarnation are basically the same idea of life after death.
items.random_wrong: Christians believe everyone is reincarnated until they accept Jesus.
items.why: Christianity affirms one bodily resurrection, not cyclical rebirth. Students flatten different afterlife concepts into a single 'life after death' idea, missing the very different metaphysics involved.

items.q: What does the Abrahamic covenant in Judaism involve?
items.correct: God's promise to Abraham of land, descendants and blessing, in return for faithfulness — the foundational covenant marking Jews as a covenant people with responsibilities, not privileges.
items.misconception: Being 'chosen' means Jews believe they are superior to other peoples.
items.random_wrong: The covenant means Jews must not interact with non-Jews.
items.why: Chosenness in Jewish theology means chosen for responsibility (following Torah, being a 'light to the nations'), not chosen for favour. Students misread it as ethnic supremacy, missing the obligation it imposes.

items.q: When was the Trinity affirmed by council?
items.why: It's AD 325. “AD 33” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Who developed the doctrine of original sin?
items.why: Correct answer: Augustine. “Aquinas” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: On what day did Jesus rise according to Christians?
items.why: The real figure is 3 day. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
